content.text: Launch Retrospective Action Items

A launch retrospective is a structured review that runs after a feature has been in production long enough for the team to have learned something — typically at or just before the T+30 post-launch review. It is not a post-mortem (which focuses on incidents) and not a sprint retrospective (which focuses on team process). A launch retrospective focuses specifically on the gap between what was planned and what was observed: what worked as expected, what did not, what the team learned, and what concrete changes should follow. The action items generated from the retrospective are the sole output that drives measurable improvement; the other three quadrants exist to inform the action items.

attrs.alt: Four-quadrant retrospective analysis: Went Well, Improvement Areas, What We Learned, Action Items

content.text: The four-quadrant format prevents retrospectives from becoming either pure celebration (listing only what went well) or pure complaint sessions (listing only what went wrong). The "What We Learned" quadrant is specific to Claude integrations: it captures model behaviour insights, user adoption patterns, and safety observations that may inform not just this integration's improvement backlog but also the team's overall prompting practices and the organisation's approach to future Claude integrations.
Structuring the Retrospective Meeting

The retrospective meeting requires preparation to be productive. Without pre-work, the meeting defaults to whoever speaks first dominating the content and the "Went Well" quadrant being longer than any other because positive observations are easier to surface unprompted than structural improvement insights. The preparation process ensures all four quadrants are populated with representative input before the team starts prioritising.
Async pre-work (T-48h):

Each team member submits 2–3 observations per quadrant asynchronously before the meeting. This surfaces input from quieter team members and prevents groupthink, where the first few observations anchor the group's thinking.
Data pull (T-24h):

The data analyst prepares the launch metrics summary: positive feedback rate trend, refusal rate, error rate, cost per interaction vs. estimate, and adoption curve. Retrospectives grounded in data produce more actionable insights than retrospectives based on memory alone.
Clustering and voting (first 20 min of meeting):

Merge duplicate observations across team members. Then dot-vote to identify which improvement areas and learnings the team considers most significant — this prevents the meeting from spending equal time on a minor inconvenience and a systematic quality problem.
Action item generation (middle 25 min):

For each top-voted improvement area and learning, the team generates at least one concrete action item. The action item must have a single owner and a due date; observations without owners and dates are not action items.
Prioritisation (last 15 min):
Rank action items by impact. The top 3 action items are committed for the next sprint; the remainder enter the improvement backlog at the appropriate priority tier.

The health_check method encodes the most common retrospective failure modes. Retrospectives that produce zero committed action items are common when the team runs out of time during prioritisation and defers the decision to a follow-up meeting that never happens. Retrospectives that produce action items without owners are common when the team is reluctant to assign work in the meeting itself; the result is a list that everyone assumes someone else will handle. The health check flags these conditions when the retrospective record is created, before the meeting ends and the team disperses.
Claude-Specific Retrospective Categories

Standard software retrospectives focus on process, planning, and technical execution. Claude integration retrospectives have additional categories that are specific to the model interaction layer. These categories should appear explicitly in the retrospective template rather than being surfaced only if a team member thinks to mention them.
Category
What to Surface
Typical Action Item
Prompt design
Instructions that needed revision after real user input arrived
Update system prompt; add to golden set
Refusal patterns
Content categories that triggered unexpected refusals at launch
Refine system prompt permissions; engage Anthropic support if model-level
Cost model accuracy
Gap between projected and actual token usage per interaction
Profile context window; reduce over-sending; revise cost model
User input distribution
How real user prompts differed from test prompts
Expand test prompt golden set with real input samples
Model version assumptions
Whether a model update changed behaviour after launch
Pin model version or add upgrade testing gate
Error handling gaps
API errors or rate limits that the integration did not handle gracefully
Add retry logic, circuit breaker, or user-visible error message

The user input distribution category is often the most valuable for first-time Claude integrations. Teams build and test their integration with a curated set of example prompts that represent what they expect users to ask; real users ask for things the team did not anticipate. The gap between the test prompt distribution and the real prompt distribution reveals implicit assumptions in the system prompt that need to be made explicit. Capturing this gap during the retrospective and expanding the golden test set with real input samples prevents the same surprises from occurring in the next sprint cycle.
Action Item Tracking After the Retrospective

The retrospective action items must be tracked to closure, not filed and forgotten. The tracking mechanism can be as simple as a shared document or as structured as the improvement backlog described in the feedback-to-backlog process. What matters is that the action items are visible, have named owners, have due dates, and are reviewed at a regular cadence. The T+30 post-launch review is the natural closure point for retrospective action items that were committed to the sprint: the review evaluates whether the completed action items improved the metrics they were intended to improve.

The t30_readiness method provides a pre-review check on whether the retrospective's committed action items are complete before the T+30 review meeting. A retrospective with no completed committed items at the T+30 review means the improvement cycle did not close: the team identified problems, made commitments, and did not follow through. This pattern — visible in the readiness check before the review rather than discovered during it — is actionable: the product manager or tech lead can follow up on overdue items in the days before the review rather than arriving at the review with nothing to show.
Prioritising Action Items into the Improvement Backlog

Not all retrospective action items are equal, and not all should be committed to the immediately following sprint. An action item that addresses a P1 quality issue affecting 40% of users is not equivalent to an action item that documents a minor process improvement for the next launch cycle. Prioritisation ensures that the team's improvement capacity is directed at the highest-impact changes first. Retrospective action items follow the same priority tier framework as feedback-driven backlog items, which allows them to be managed in the same backlog without creating a separate queue.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class ActionItemCategory(str, Enum):
    PROMPT_ENGINEERING   = "prompt_engineering"    # changes to system prompt or conversation structure
    ERROR_HANDLING       = "error_handling"        # API error recovery, fallbacks, circuit breakers
    MONITORING           = "monitoring"            # alerting, dashboards, runbook updates
    COST_OPTIMISATION    = "cost_optimisation"     # token reduction, context trimming
    PROCESS              = "process"               # team workflow, testing, documentation
    MODEL_MANAGEMENT     = "model_management"      # version pinning, upgrade testing gates
    USER_EXPERIENCE      = "user_experience"       # UI, error messages, loading states

PRIORITY_RULES: dict[ActionItemCategory, str] = {
    ActionItemCategory.PROMPT_ENGINEERING:   "P1 if refusal rate > threshold; P2 otherwise",
    ActionItemCategory.ERROR_HANDLING:       "P1 if error rate > 1%; P2 if intermittent",
    ActionItemCategory.MONITORING:           "P2 if gap caused delayed detection; P3 otherwise",
    ActionItemCategory.COST_OPTIMISATION:    "P2 if cost > 125% of estimate; P3 otherwise",
    ActionItemCategory.PROCESS:              "P3 default; P2 if blocked future launch",
    ActionItemCategory.MODEL_MANAGEMENT:     "P2 if model update caused regression; P3 otherwise",
    ActionItemCategory.USER_EXPERIENCE:      "P2 if impacted task completion; P3 otherwise",
}

@dataclass
class PrioritisedActionItem:
    action_id: str
    description: str
    category: ActionItemCategory
    priority: str
    owner: str
    target_sprint: str    # e.g. "sprint-47"
    impact_evidence: str  # what data supports this priority level

    def priority_justification(self) -> str:
        rule = PRIORITY_RULES.get(self.category, "no rule defined")
        return f"{self.category.value}: {rule} → assigned {self.priority}. Evidence: {self.impact_evidence}"

The PRIORITY_RULES dictionary makes the priority assignment transparent and reproducible. When a team member questions why a prompt engineering fix was assigned P2 rather than P1, the rule says it should be P1 only if the refusal rate exceeds the threshold — if the threshold was not exceeded, P2 is correct. This prevents priority creep (every item getting bumped to P1 because it feels important to the person who identified it) and priority undervaluation (items being assigned P3 because the engineer who raised them was being conservative about their own workload). Both distortions are common in retrospectives without an explicit prioritisation framework.
Building a Lessons-Learned Registry Across Launches

A single retrospective's "What We Learned" quadrant is valuable for the current feature. A registry of lessons across all launches is valuable for the entire engineering organisation: it tells teams starting a new Claude integration what problems their colleagues encountered and how they resolved them, without requiring those teams to repeat the same experiments. The registry is most valuable for the Claude-specific categories — prompt design, refusal patterns, cost model accuracy — where institutional knowledge is not available from generic software engineering resources.

The to_knowledge_article method produces a human-readable format that can be published in an internal wiki or knowledge base. The outcome field is intentionally left pending until after the action has been applied and evaluated — a lesson whose action was taken but did not resolve the issue is as valuable as a lesson whose action worked, because it prevents other teams from repeating the same unsuccessful fix. Both types of lessons should be in the registry with their outcomes filled in after the fact.
Summary

Launch retrospectives generate the action items that close the improvement loop between a feature's launch and its T+30 post-launch review. The four-quadrant format (Went Well, Improvement Areas, What We Learned, Action Items) ensures the retrospective captures both positive and negative observations and converts them into concrete commitments. The LaunchRetrospective and RetroActionItem data structures encode the retrospective record in a queryable format that supports the T+30 readiness check and the backlog prioritisation process. Claude integration retrospectives include six domain-specific categories — prompt design, refusal patterns, cost model accuracy, user input distribution, model version assumptions, and error handling gaps — that surface concerns unique to the model interaction layer. The ActionItemTracker provides a pre-T+30 readiness check that identifies overdue commitments before the review meeting rather than discovering them during it.
